title: The Modern Corporation and Private Property
rationale: The foundational text of corporate governance. Berle and Means's empirical demonstration that ownership and control had separated in the 200 largest US corporations reshaped all subsequent law, economics, and public policy thinking about the corporation. Selected as Book #1 for the corporate-and-commercial-law leaf because no other work of legal scholarship has had comparable impact on corporate law doctrine, securities regulation, or the theory of the firm.
excerpt: The foundational study that revealed how the modern corporation had divorced ownership from control, transforming private property and reshaping the architecture of American capitalism.
keyIdeas: The separation of ownership and control defines the modern corporation — shareholders own shares but managers exercise real power
The 200 largest US corporations controlled 45–53% of non-banking corporate wealth by 1930, concentrating economic power unprecedented in history
Passive shareholders with 'rational apathy' cannot effectively monitor dispersed managerial authority
Corporate powers must be exercised as powers in trust, subject to equitable constraints on behalf of all constituencies
Concentration of economic power in corporations demands new forms of public accountability and legal regulation
keyTakeaways: Agency theory's origins lie here: managers (agents) do not automatically act in shareholders' (principals') interests
Proxy voting, non-voting shares, and restricted removal of directors systematically strip shareholder power
The book justified and shaped the 1933 Securities Act and the 1934 Securities Exchange Act
Berle and Means argued corporations are social institutions, not mere private property arrangements
whoShouldRead: Corporate lawyers and legal scholars studying governance
Economists interested in institutional economics and the origins of agency theory
Business historians examining the rise of managerial capitalism
Policymakers and regulators involved in securities and corporate law
whoShouldSkip: Practitioners seeking current Sarbanes-Oxley or Dodd-Frank compliance guidance
Readers looking for light, popular business writing — this is a dense scholarly text
